steps.title: Identify the relationship the blank must express
steps.content:
Clinical trials "fail to [blank]" conclusions practitioners reach from real-world observations. One reason: practitioners may overlook confounding variables — meaning their conclusions can be wrong or incomplete. The blank must mean something like "confirm" or "support."
  • Choice A — 'circumvent': means to get around or bypass; trial results can't bypass a conclusion ✗
  • Choice B — 'corroborate': means to confirm or support with evidence; trials that find different results "fail to corroborate" practitioners' conclusions ✓
  • Choice C — 'disseminate': means to spread widely; trial results don't spread conclusions ✗
  • Choice D — 'implement': means to put into effect; trial results don't enact conclusions ✗

steps.title: Confirm with context
steps.content:
When practitioners overlook confounding variables, their conclusions about what caused an outcome can be flawed. A rigorous clinical trial controls for those variables — so it would naturally produce different results and "fail to corroborate" the practitioner's observation.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
